metadata.title: Why Doing Homework With AI Makes You Worse at the Test
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Claude Code for Students candidate 01. Source chapter: claude-code-for-students/chapters/01-homework-quiz-gap.md (the fluency trap / desirable difficulties mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: CRIMSON = delegation / atrophy / the fluency trap; INK (plain) = struggle / consolidation / durable skill. One accent. Never swap mid-film.
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO deep neuroscience of synaptic plasticity, NO formal statistics on effect size beyond the research-grounding cold open, NO history of cognitive-load theory, NO Bjork full desirable-difficulties taxonomy. Mechanism only: delegation removes the struggle event; without that event, no encoding occurs; six weeks later the skill is absent.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B14: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. D finishes a six-function problem set in 18 minutes using Claude. He scores a hundred. Then the laptops go away. Same material, simpler quiz. He stares at his screen for 12 minutes and scores a 41.

This is not a one-off. A thousand Turkish high school students did their math practice either by hand or with unrestricted GPT-4. At the end, same unassisted exam. The AI group had scored 48 percent higher on practice. They scored 17 points lower on the test.

production_viz.label: practice vs exam: AI group vs control
production_viz.mechanic: two bar pairs side by side — Practice bars (AI group taller, labeled +48%) and Exam bars (AI group shorter, labeled -17pts). Bars animate in. The gap arrow then appears.
production_viz.note: Bastani et al. 2025. Illustrative bar heights; exact percentages are factual.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] bar chart: practice vs exam gap

beats.narration_text: Practice should predict exam performance. The AI group's practice scores predicted higher performance. Their exam scores were dramatically lower. Why?

card.sub: here it predicted the opposite — why?

beats.narration_text: When you sit with a problem you can't solve, you pull up fragments of what you know, combine them, notice the failure, and try again. That cycle of try, fail, adjust — that is uncomfortable. It is also the event that does the work.

production_viz.label: the struggle cycle: try -> fail -> adjust -> consolidation
production_viz.mechanic: three nodes arrange in a loop: TRY, FAIL, ADJUST. Arrows draw on between them. Then a fourth node CONSOLIDATION appears connected below ADJUST with a bold arrow.
production_viz.note: all in INK (good/kept); no crimson yet

beats.narration_text: Claude gives you the clean solution. You read it. It makes sense — you follow the logic step by step and you nod. That sensation is comprehension. It is not the sensation of learning.

card.copy: Following correct code feels like learning.

beats.narration_text: Your brain encodes the two events differently. Following correct code is comprehension — you track the argument while it is playing. Writing wrong code and fixing it is an encoding event — your brain is generating candidates, checking them, revising the model. Outputs on the screen look identical. The version of you six weeks later is not.

production_viz.label: Path A: struggle -> consolidation -> durable skill; Path B: delegation -> fluent output -> atrophy
production_viz.mechanic: two horizontal paths draw on left to right. Path A in INK: STRUGGLE -> CONSOLIDATION -> SKILL. Path B in CRIMSON: DELEGATION -> OUTPUT -> ATROPHY. Label chips mark each path.
production_viz.note: the core visual object from the card

beats.narration_text: Bjork called this the illusion of competence. The output in front of you is competent. The output's competence belongs to the model. Your competence will be measured when the screen is closed and the quiz begins.
beats.scene_description: a student at a desk, the AI chat window open and glowing, the student's expression calm and satisfied — the fluency trap moment; editorial flat treatment on newsprint ground
beats.image_prompt: B07, student at a desk with a laptop screen showing a clean code solution, looking satisfied and nodding, pen idle on a blank notebook beside the keyboard, late evening lighting, editorial flat desaturated treatment

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL ai] the fluency trap moment

beats.narration_text: Maya pastes a sorting algorithm into Claude. She reads the clean solution, nods, moves on. A 100 on the homework. Three weeks later, closed laptops, same algorithm in a slightly different context. Maya writes for i in range, crosses it out, sits still. The skill was never hers. It lived in Claude for 30 seconds and she handed it back.

production_viz.label: Maya's timeline: paste -> nod -> 100 ... quiz: blank page
production_viz.mechanic: horizontal timeline draws on left to right. Three nodes: PASTE CODE (crimson chip), READ + NOD, SCORE 100. Then a gap (labeled '3 weeks'), then QUIZ: blank page with a partial line of code crossed out.
production_viz.note: illustrative example — labeled as such in FACTCHECK

beats.narration_text: The Anthropic researchers found that the engineers who scored lowest did one of three things: they handed the whole task to Claude and moved on, they started alone and let Claude take over the moment it got hard, or they pasted errors into Claude in a loop — never building a model of why the code broke.

production_viz.label: three low-score patterns: delegation, progressive reliance, iterative debugging
production_viz.mechanic: three stacked rows draw on. Each is a chip label in crimson followed by a short serif description. DELEGATION: hand whole task, move on. PROGRESSIVE RELIANCE: start alone, hand off at friction. ITERATIVE DEBUGGING: paste error, accept fix, repeat.

beats.narration_text: The contrast group — engineers who scored above 65 percent — asked questions about the library before asking Claude to write code in it. They used the model like a colleague who had read the documentation. They wrote their own implementation first, then asked Claude to critique it.

card.sub: what the high scorers did differently

beats.narration_text: Production tasks, you can fake — Claude builds the thing and the output is indistinguishable from the output of someone who built it themselves. Diagnostic tasks you cannot fake, because diagnostic tasks require an internal model: why is this code doing what it is doing? And that model is exactly what delegation does not build.

production_viz.label: production vs diagnostic: delegation fakes the first, fails the second
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: PRODUCTION (left, chip in INK with a checkmark label 'delegation works') and DIAGNOSTIC (right, chip in CRIMSON with label 'delegation fails'). Below each, a serif line explains why. The DIAGNOSTIC column animates in last and is the heavier emphasis.
production_viz.note: diagnostic = requires internal model; production = can be faked

beats.narration_text: Student A designs a library schema by hand — gets it wrong, notices co-authored books break her model, rewrites it. Student B pastes the assignment into Claude, gets a clean schema with soft-delete columns he didn't think of. Both score an A. Six weeks later: Student A draws entities for a social-media app from habit. Student B writes Users table, Posts table, and stops. He doesn't know whether likes are a column or a join table. He saw that decision six weeks ago. He never made it.

production_viz.label: Student A vs Student B: same grade, different six-weeks-later
production_viz.mechanic: two side-by-side columns. Left (INK): STUDENT A -- draws entities, finds bug, fixes it, gets A. Right (CRIMSON): STUDENT B -- pastes to Claude, gets clean schema, gets A. Then a horizontal rule labeled 6 WEEKS LATER. Left: A draws entities on new domain from habit. Right: B writes two table names and stops. Both visible simultaneously.
production_viz.note: composite example drawn from chapter — labeled illustrative in FACTCHECK

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two students, same grade, different six weeks

beats.narration_text: The move is simple. Before you ask Claude to build anything in a domain, spend ten minutes asking Claude questions about the domain. What does this class do? What would change if I removed this dependency? What are three ways to break this? You are not asking Claude to write anything. You are building the internal model that diagnostic tasks require.

card.sub: every time. even when you think you know.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE PRACTICE — questions before code

beats.narration_text: The screen closes. The quiz begins. The tool is gone. What remains is what you built when you were building — not what Claude produced while you watched.

card.copy: Borrowed capability disappears when the tool does.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — the screen closes
